name: Boca del Infierno Loop via Roman Road and Senda de los Ganchos
name_original: Boca del Infierno – circular de trail running por la Calzada Romana y la Senda de Los Ganchos – Valle de Hecho
description: The Boca del Infierno Loop via Roman Road and Senda de los Ganchos is a difficult 4.9-mile jogging trail with 1304 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Jogging routes around Valle De Hecho are set within the Aragonese Pyrenees, offering a diverse landscape ideal for running. The region is characterized by dense forests of beech, silver fir, and pine, dramatic gorges like Boca del Infierno, and high mountain meadows. Runners will find varied terrain, from ancient Roman roads to paths alongside meandering rivers and glacial lakes. Best jogging routes around Valle De Hecho The most popular jogging route is Castillo d'Acher - trail running from Selva de Oza, an 8.0 miles (12.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 36 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through dense forest and offers views of rocky peaks. Another top favourite among local runners is Boca del Infierno Loop via Roman Road and Senda de los Ganchos, a difficult 4.9 miles (8.0 km) path. This route navigates a deep gorge and includes sections of an ancient Roman road. Local runners also love the Ibón de Acherito loop from GR11, a 6.3 miles (10.1 km) trail leading through high mountain terrain to a glacial lake, often completed in about 1 hour 53 minutes. Jogging around Valle De Hecho is defined by dense forests, dramatic gorges, and high mountain glacial lakes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from challenging trail runs to routes through picturesque valleys. The routes in Valle De Hecho are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from 2 reviews. More than 500 runners have used komoot to explore Valle De Hecho's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running routes in Valle de Hecho?
A: Valle de Hecho offers a diverse range of terrain for runners. You'll find paths through dense forests of beech, silver fir, and pine, dramatic gorges like the Boca del Infierno, and expansive high-mountain meadows. Some routes follow ancient Roman roads, while others lead alongside meandering rivers and to stunning glacial lakes. The landscape includes varied forest and mountain paths, ensuring options for different preferences.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in Valle de Hecho?
A: Yes, several excellent circular running routes are available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950314586/boca-del-infierno-loop-via-roman-road-and-senda-de-los-ganchos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boca del Infierno Loop via Roman Road and Senda de los Ganchos&lt;/a&gt; offers a challenging 4.9-mile run through a deep gorge. Another popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41521963" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibón de Acherito loop from GR11&lt;/a&gt;, a 6.3-mile trail leading to a beautiful glacial lake.

Q: What natural attractions can I see while jogging in Valle de Hecho?
A: The running routes in Valle de Hecho are rich with natural beauty. You can encounter the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/804178" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boca del Infierno Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, explore the pristine &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5017237" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibón de Acherito&lt;/a&gt; glacial lake, or enjoy the unique meandering patterns of the Aragón Subordán river in the Valle de Aguas Tuertas. The region also features dense forests, clear waterfalls, and impressive peaks like Peña Forca.

Q: Are there any challenging trail running options in the region?
A: Absolutely. Valle de Hecho is known for its challenging trail runs. A prime example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e949704367/castillo-d-acher-trail-running-from-selva-de-oza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo d'Acher - trail running from Selva de Oza&lt;/a&gt;, an 8.0-mile route with significant elevation gain through dense forest, offering views of rocky peaks. Many of the routes in the area are rated as difficult, providing a good test for experienced runners.

Q: What do other runners think about the trails in Valle de Hecho?
A: The running routes in Valle de Hecho are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars. Runners often praise the diverse and stunning natural landscapes, from dense forests to dramatic gorges and high mountain lakes, which provide a unique and rewarding experience.

Q: Can I find routes that offer historical insights while running?
A: Yes, you can combine your run with a touch of history. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950314586/boca-del-infierno-loop-via-roman-road-and-senda-de-los-ganchos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boca del Infierno Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, includes sections of an ancient Roman road, adding a unique historical dimension to your outdoor activity.

Q: Are there any running routes that pass by lakes?
A: Yes, the region is home to beautiful glacial lakes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41521963" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibón de Acherito loop from GR11&lt;/a&gt; is a fantastic option, leading directly to the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5017237" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibón de Acherito&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable lakes in the area include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5586805" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estanés Lake&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2691256" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lake Ansabère&lt;/a&gt;, which can be reached via various trails.

Q: What is the best season for running in Valle de Hecho?
A: Valle de Hecho offers excellent running opportunities throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, while summer allows access to higher mountain trails and glacial lakes. Winter can be challenging due to snow, especially at higher elevations, but lower valley routes may still be accessible.

Q: Are there any long-distance running trails in Valle de Hecho?
A: For those seeking longer distances, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/26267858" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo d'Acher – Achar of Aguas Tuertas loop from Caseta de guardabosques&lt;/a&gt; cover over 15 miles (25 km) and involve significant elevation changes, providing a substantial challenge for long-distance runners.

Q: What are the starting points for many running routes in the area?
A: Many routes, particularly those leading into the high mountains, often start from areas like Guarrinza, which features expansive high-mountain meadows. The Selva de Oza is another popular starting point, offering access to trails through dense forests and towards rocky peaks.

Q: Are there any routes suitable for families or those looking for a gentler run?
A: While many routes are challenging, the Valle de Hecho also offers options for a gentler jog. The region's diverse landscape includes 'endless meadows' and paths along rivers that can be suitable for a more relaxed pace, though specific 'easy' routes are less common in the provided data. It's best to check individual route details for elevation and distance to find the most suitable option.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Valle de Hecho?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many trails in natural parks in Spain, but it's always advisable to keep them on a leash, especially given the presence of diverse wildlife, including brown bears and various bird species. Always check local regulations or signage at trailheads for specific rules regarding pets in certain areas or protected zones within the Valles Occidentales Natural Park.

faqs.question: Where does the Boca del Infierno Loop start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The loop typically starts near the village of Hecho in the Valle de Hecho. While specific parking details aren't provided, trailheads in this region often have designated parking areas. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The trail is located within the Natural Park of the Western Valleys. While dogs are generally allowed in natural parks, they should always be kept on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all users. Please respect park regulations.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to experience this loop?
faqs.answer: The trail offers diverse scenery throughout the year. Autumn is particularly beautiful due to the abundant beech trees, which display vibrant colors. Spring and early summer also provide pleasant conditions. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out, especially for alpine sections.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this trail or the Natural Park?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are mentioned for accessing the Boca del Infierno Loop or the Natural Park of the Western Valleys. However, it's always good practice to respect park rules and regulations.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this jogging route?
faqs.answer: Expect a challenging and varied terrain. The route includes unpaved paths (approx. 4.56 km) and alpine sections (approx. 3.21 km), with only a small paved portion. You'll encounter hiking paths, mountain hiking paths, and other trails. The Roman Road section has a moderate uphill slope, while the Senda de los Ganchos involves significant elevation gain and can include large boulders, requiring sure-footedness. Some sections can be narrow.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The highlight is the dramatic Boca del Infierno gorge, known for its narrow passages, sculpted limestone, and waterfalls. You'll also traverse the ancient Roman Road, pass by the abandoned San Juan de Dios Campground, and see the 16th-century defensive tower, Torre Lo Vixia.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, the loop incorporates sections of several other well-known paths. You'll find parts of the Senda de Camille 9 (Oza-Gabardito por Vía Romana), Variante Tacheras – Oza – Ref. Gabardito – Ref. de Lizara – Canfranc Estación – Canal Roya, Viejo Camino de Santiago por el Valle de Hecho - Calzada del Summo Pyreneo, GR-11.1 Calzada Romana, and the Senda de Los Ganchos.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging route?
faqs.answer: For jogging, this 7.96 km (4.95 miles) loop typically takes around 1 hour and 19 minutes to complete. However, this is an estimate, and your actual time may vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Is there a recommended direction to tackle the loop?
faqs.answer: Some sources suggest that for hiking, an easier direction is to go uphill via the Senda de los Ganchos and then downhill on the Roman Road. For jogging, the overall difficulty remains high, but this might offer a more manageable ascent before a potentially faster descent.

faqs.question: What should I wear or bring for this jogging route?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating and varied terrain, sturdy trail running shoes with good grip are essential. Layers of clothing are recommended, as conditions can change. Bring sufficient water, snacks, a map or GPS device, and consider trekking poles for stability on steeper or rockier sections.

faqs.question: Is this loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This loop is generally considered a difficult jogging route, requiring very good fitness, sure-footedness, and sturdy shoes. It involves significant elevation changes (397m up, 390m down) over challenging terrain. It is best suited for experienced trail runners rather than beginners.
